original_abstract:
Modern high-performance object detectors increasingly rely on detection proposals to narrow the search space, replacing traditional exhaustive sliding window techniques. However, despite their integration into state-of-the-art frameworks, the specific trade-offs inherent in different proposal generation methods remain poorly understood. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, evaluating them across several critical dimensions: repeatability, ground truth recall on PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets, and their downstream impact on DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN detector performance.

Our empirical findings demonstrate that for effective object detection, improving the localization accuracy of a proposal is just as vital as maximizing high recall. To capture this relationship, we introduce Average Recall (AR), a novel metric that simultaneously rewards both coverage and spatial precision. We show that AR correlates remarkably well with final detector accuracy, far outperforming traditional metrics like recall at a single overlap threshold. By identifying the systematic strengths and weaknesses of existing approaches, this work provides a unified framework and actionable insights for practitioners to select and fine-tune proposal methods for a variety of computer vision tasks.
humanized_abstract:
Current state-of-the-art high-performance object detectors employ detection proposals to eliminate unnecessary search space via pruning, which substitutes the slow but exhaustive sliding window approach. Despite being included within a number of leading detection frameworks, it is still unclear what the underlying trade-offs are between different methods of proposal generation. In this work, we analyze 12 different proposal methods and 4 baselines along several metrics such as repeatability, as well as ground truth recall on PASCAL, ImageNet and MS COCO, to name a few, and finally the performance of the DPM, R-CNN and Fast R-CNN detection algorithms.

In this work we provide experimental evidence that both the precision of classification and the precision of proposal localization should be increased to improve object detection. In other words, it is as important to have precise proposals as it is to have high recall. We then define a new performance metric named Average Recall (AR) that takes into account both aspects. Our experimental results show that average recall correlates very highly with the performance of a detection algorithm and that it is better than any other evaluation metric using a single overlap threshold. Our goal in this work is to understand and expose the properties of proposal methods, providing a unifying perspective that will aid practitioners in choosing and fine-tuning proposal networks for diverse computer vision applications.

In this work we provide experimental evidence that both the precision of classification and the precision of proposal localization should be increased to improve object detection. document.input:
